text: The Chinook database is a relational model of a digital media store.
The Artist table contains a unique ArtistId and the artist's Name.
The Album table stores music albums and is identified by AlbumId.
Every record in the Album table has a Title and an associated ArtistId.
The Track table is the central entity, uniquely identified by TrackId.
Each track has a Name, an AlbumId, and a MediaTypeId.
The Genre table maps a GenreId to a specific music genre Name.
The MediaType table defines formats using MediaTypeId and Name.
The Employee table manages staff data, identified by EmployeeId.
Employees have a LastName, FirstName, and a Title (e.g., General Manager).
The Customer table tracks store users via a unique CustomerId.
Customers are associated with a SupportRepId, which links to the Employee table.
The Invoice table records transactions using a unique InvoiceId.
Every invoice is tied to a CustomerId and has an InvoiceDate.
The InvoiceLine table stores individual items in an invoice via InvoiceLineId.
InvoiceLine records link to both an InvoiceId and a TrackId.
The Playlist table groups tracks and is identified by PlaylistId.
The PlaylistTrack table is a join table for many-to-many relationships between playlists and tracks.
All primary keys in Chinook are typically Integers.
String data in Chinook (like names and titles) is stored in VARCHAR columns.
The Album table has a foreign key ArtistId referencing the Artist table.
The Track table has a foreign key AlbumId referencing the Album table.
The Track table references the Genre table via the GenreId foreign key.
The Track table references the MediaType table via the MediaTypeId foreign key.
The Invoice table references the Customer table via the CustomerId foreign key.
The InvoiceLine table references the Invoice table via the InvoiceId foreign key.
The InvoiceLine table references the Track table via the TrackId foreign key.
The Customer table references the Employee table via the SupportRepId.
The Employee table has a self-referencing foreign key ReportsTo pointing to another EmployeeId.
The PlaylistTrack table contains a composite relationship between PlaylistId and TrackId.
Deleting an Artist requires handling associated Albums due to foreign key constraints.
A single Album can contain multiple Tracks, all sharing the same AlbumId.
A single Artist can have multiple Albums in the Album table.
A Customer can have multiple Invoices over time.
An Invoice can consist of multiple InvoiceLine entries.
Each Track belongs to exactly one Genre.
Each Track is associated with exactly one Media Type.
Multiple Playlists can contain the same TrackId.
An Employee can support multiple Customers as a Support Representative.
The database uses standard relational integrity to prevent orphaned records.
The Track table includes a Composer column for the song's creator.
Track length is stored in the Milliseconds column.
The size of a track file is stored in the Bytes column.
Each track has a UnitPrice, usually set to 0.99.
The Invoice table contains a Total column representing the final amount.
Billing details in Invoice include BillingAddress, BillingCity, and BillingState.
The Invoice table tracks location via BillingCountry and BillingPostalCode.
Customer records include contact info: Address, City, State, and Country.
The Customer table stores Phone, Fax, and Email addresses.
Employees have a BirthDate and a HireDate stored in DateTime format.
Employee contact info includes Address, City, State, Country, and PostalCode.
The InvoiceLine table tracks the Quantity of each track purchased.
The InvoiceLine table stores a UnitPrice at the time of sale.
The Customer table has a Company field for corporate clients.
Playlists are identified by a simple Name string.
Genres include 'Rock', 'Jazz', 'Metal', and 'Classical'.
Media types include 'MPEG audio file' and 'Protected AAC audio file'.
Employee titles include 'Sales Support Agent' and 'IT Staff'.
Invoices are sorted chronologically by InvoiceDate.
The Total in an invoice is the sum of UnitPrice * Quantity for all its lines.
To find an artist's tracks, you must join Artist to Album and then to Track.
Calculating total sales per country requires joining Customer and Invoice.
To see what a customer bought, join Customer, Invoice, InvoiceLine, and Track.
Finding the most popular genre involves counting InvoiceLine entries grouped by GenreId.
Calculating the total duration of a playlist requires summing Milliseconds in Track.
Determining which employee is most successful requires summing Invoice.Total via Customer.SupportRepId.
The ReportsTo column in Employee establishes a management hierarchy.
A customer with no SupportRepId is technically possible but rare in this schema.
Top-selling tracks are identified by the frequency of their TrackId in InvoiceLine.
Revenue by year is calculated using the strftime or Year function on InvoiceDate.
The 'Rock' genre typically has the highest count of tracks in the Chinook database.
Media types often distinguish between audio and video content.
The PlaylistTrack table allows a track to exist in zero or many playlists.
An invoice is always linked to exactly one customer.
An invoice line is always linked to exactly one track.
Customer emails are unique identifiers for communication but not the primary key.
Postal codes are stored as strings to accommodate international formats.
All prices are stored as Numeric or Decimal types for accuracy.
The ArtistId in the Artist table is an auto-incrementing integer.
The Album table Title can be up to 160 characters long.
The Track table Name column can be up to 200 characters.
Some tracks may have a NULL Composer field.
The Employee table includes a Phone, Fax, and Email for internal use.
The MediaType name describes the technical codec of the file.
The Invoice table Total column is a redundant but helpful summary of InvoiceLine costs.
There are 11 primary tables in the standard Chinook schema.
The Playlist table name can be '90’s Music' or 'TV Shows'.
An artist's name might be a person or a band.
The Customer table FirstName and LastName are separate columns.
A Customer can be from various countries like Germany, Brazil, or Canada.
The Employee hierarchy usually has one 'General Manager' at the top.
InvoiceLineId ensures every item on every receipt is uniquely trackable.
Joining Track and Genre is a many-to-one relationship.
The Album to Artist relationship is many-to-one.
PlaylistTrack handles the many-to-many relationship between Playlist and Track.
The database allows for tracks with a UnitPrice of zero (rare but possible).
InvoiceDate includes both date and time components.
Every InvoiceLine must have an InvoiceId.
The schema is designed to prevent duplicate tracks within the same album.
The Chinook database serves as a complete blueprint for a digital music retail system.
